availability.p:
Published under a
Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike 4.0 International (CC BY-NC-SA 4.0)

editorialDecl.p: Encoding of this text has followed the recommendations of the LombardPress 1.0.0 guidelines for a diplomatic edition.

change.p: Created file for the first time.

div.head: Chapter 28b [Bnf 12926 Translation]
div.p: ¶ Where, therefore, unbelievers committing such a crime (namely) killing infants for sacrifice or for eating are found: they are not always to be attacked with war, although it pertains to the Church to provide a remedy by removing the impediment: but first and much must be weighed: lest, in order to prevent the death of a few innocents, we devote to destruction an innumerable multitude of people, even those undeserving: we destroy whole kingdoms: we implant hatred of the Christian religion in their souls: so that nowhere would they be willing to hear the name or teaching of Christ forever. Which things are certainly contrary to the end which God and Mother Church herself intend.

Indeed, one must abstain from war and tolerate that evil at least for a time: indeed even perpetually in certain cases: as will be said: but rather some prudent and Christian method must be devised: by which through the word of God cleansing all things, or through admonitions, prayers, or exhortations, the peoples might abstain from that barbarity. For this custom does not prevail among all the peoples of the Indies: nor if it prevails will there be a great multitude of those killed: otherwise they would all already have been consumed. And yet we find all regions most full of mortals.

That one must abstain from war and even tolerate the death of a few innocents
is proven by arguments and by manifold authority.

And first indeed thus. For according to the rules of right reason, when we are between two evils both of fault and of punishment, and we cannot avoid both: we ought to choose the lesser evil. For with respect to the greater, the lesser evil has the nature of good. Thus teaches
the Philosopher
in the second book of the Ethics, chapter 9 . and
third Ethics chapter 5 . And
fifth Ethics chapter 6 . And
third Topics . It is likewise proven from the text
c. duo mala and following, distinction 13 . the
canonists note, and distinction 14, c. 1 . and in
c. non solum, question 4, case 22 . and
by the legists in l. 1. §. penultimo. ff. de periculo . and
commodo rei vendie . and in
l. quotiens la. 4a. ff. de regulis . Now the lesser evil is the death of a few innocents than an innumerable multitude of people perishing forever, slaughtered by the fury of war.

¶ Likewise it is better or a lesser evil than that whole kingdoms, cities, and fortresses be utterly destroyed. For not all eat those innocent victims: but only the princes or priests who sacrifice them: but war drags along with it into destruction innumerable innocent people who have merited nothing of the sort: therefore, if by no other way than war can those evils be removed: one must abstain from it and such evils must be tolerated.

¶ Moreover, it is without comparison a lesser inconvenience that a few innocents die: than that the adorable name of Christ be blasphemed by unbelievers: and the Christian religion be defamed and held in hatred
by those peoples and by others to whom the report flies: when they have heard that by Christians, very many of their people - children, old men, women - are slaughtered without cause, as necessarily happens, indeed has happened, in the fury of war.

What I ask will this be: if not to be a perpetual obstacle to their salvation: so that there will be no further hope for their conversion? Therefore, in the case where war ought to be waged concerning such matters: it is rather to be allowed that those few innocents be oppressed or suffer unjust death. Indeed, it would be the greatest sin and against the natural law on this account to wage war against unbelievers. Which is proven thus. According to right reason, and thus it is established to be from the natural law, in every case and in every matter, from two evils especially of fault: one must assumeere what harms less or is believed will harm less: therefore, to wish to liberate innocents through war, given the case as posited in its terms as it has been posited, will be against the natural law and a sin not only mortal but indeed most grave.

This is clear. because the graver the sin is: the greater the harm it inflicts, according to
St. Thomas
1a 2ae, question 20, article 5 . and
question 73, article 8 in the body . And this is so even if that harm is not the intention nor foreseen, because whatever per se follows from a sin: pertains in some way to the very species of the sin. But from such a war an infinite multitude of people
of both sexes and ages, all innocent, will necessarily perish in both worlds: and the other evils which have been related will per se follow upon the war itself. Therefore, those would commitur a most grave mortal sin ifwho by the way of war would attemptur to liberate those suffering such evils.

This reasoning is confirmed by the rules which the learned jurists hand down concerning permission. Which permission is indeed due: when evils and even grave sins are permitted so that more grave ones in the republic may be avoided: or lest goods by which the state of the republic is made more firm be impeded. This is clear from the permission of prostitutes in cities: and in the rites of the Jews. Whence the prince or governor of any republic in the posited case ought by law to tolerate evil: from which the utilitytem of the republic is hoped to ariseturam, or a coming evil is prevented, which if he did not do: he would certainly sin mortally.

These things are especially proven
distinction 50, vt constitueretur . and
c. domino sancto . And in
c. at si clerici in § de adultrerijs de judicis . and in
c. consilium. de obseruatione jeiuniorum . and in
c. denique. distinction 4 . And in other parts. And
Johannes Andreae
discourses very extensively on permission in regula peccatum. de regulis iuris, question 3 . And the most learned master
Vitoria
in the second relectio on the Indies
proves this our opinion, specifically speaking of not waging war against the Indians.

¶ Secondly, the aforesaid conclusion is proven
principally thus: we have a negative precept which must be observed in every case: nor is it licit in any way or place to transgress it, namely
thou shalt not kill . This is read in
Exodus chapter 20 . Again in
chapter 23 of that book
there is another stricter one:
the innocent and just (says God) you shall not kill, because I abhor the impious . Likewise
Daniel 13 . Hence
St. Thomas
in the third book of the Sentences, distinction 47, article 3, ad 1 .
To hand over an innocent man to suffering and death against his will is impious and cruel , thus he says.

For to the laws which God wished to be observed inviolably and strictly He added threats against transgressors: which is a vehement indication: that the Lord wishes them to be observed most exactly, according to that saying:
You shall not harm the widow and the orphan: if you afflict them, they will cry out to me: and I will hear their cry: and my fury will be kindled, and I will strike you with the sword, and your wives shall be widows and your sons orphans , et cetera.
Exodus as above . Again, we have another negative precept par excellence in
Deuteronomy 24 .
The fathers shall not be put to death for the children: nor the children for the fathers: but each one shall die for his own sin .

But if wars are waged against the Indians for this cause: a huge multitude of blameless and most innocent people will perish, utterly immune
from those crimes: and the son will bear the iniquity of the father: and the father the iniquity of the son. Therefore, such a war is impious, and the warriors together with those commanding, giving counsel, or giving aid to wage it are pursued by God with mortal hatred.

¶ And because
a little
above we said there is another stricter precept, namely
the innocent and just you shall not kill .
Exodus 23 , for clearer explanation it should be noted: that according to
St. Thomas
quodlibet 9, article 15 . and
Henry of Ghent
quodlibet 2, article 17 , four genera of human acts are found. Some are absolutely good, so that in no way can they be evil, such as to adore, worship, and love God and neighbor on account of God.

Some are absolutely and properly impious and foul, so that in no way can they be good, but they have inseparable depravity and impiety according to
the Philosopher
in the second book of the Ethics, chapter [9], whence they can never be licit. Of this kind are adulteries, ingratitude, theft, and also lying, for indeed it is depraved in itself and to be avoided
according to him in the fourth book of his work .

Some, considered absolutely, bear a certain deformity and seem foul: but with an added quality or circumstance they are licit. Of this kind are to stripto kill or to strike a man, which considered absolutely shows disorder and deformity: however, if you add that a judge hangs a robber: he acts piously and
obtains the grace of Christ for himself. But certain human acts are indifferent (that is) which are neither evil nor good, for instance to go to the forum.

Therefore, to the second genus pertains killing innocents: which can never be licit from any accident, on the authority of
St. Thomas
2a 2ae, question 64, article 6 in the body, at the end . Where he concludes that it is in no way licit to kill an innocent, and in
question 100, article 8, ad 3
he says that human law cannot grant that a good man be killed unjustly. And
Abulensis
on Genesis, chapter 22, column 6, at the beginning and at the end : says that to kill an innocent is always evil: nor in any way can it be made good.

By which it appears that this precept was most strict: and by no reasoning or circumstance can it ever be licit by human law or by a prince's command to kill the blameless and harmless: in order that other innocents be freed from death, especially where those who are killed unjustly, as is in our case, are few: and those to be killed, that they may be freed, are many.
